source.title: Convergent but temporally separated inputs to lateral amygdala neurons from the auditory thalamus and auditory cortex use different postsynaptic receptors: in vivo intracellular and extracellular recordings in fear conditioning pathways

confidence_rationale: Li et al. demonstrate that the thalamic (low-road) and cortical (high-road) inputs to the lateral amygdala are not only parallel but mechanistically distinct: the thalamic pathway engages NMDA receptors in addition to AMPA, while the cortical pathway uses only AMPA. This means the two streams carry qualitatively different computational properties — the thalamic route can integrate signals over time via NMDA's voltage-dependent gating. For ARC-027, this deepens the case: the HARM stream (thalamic route) is not just anatomically parallel to E1 but uses a different synaptic mechanism, consistent with it being a distinct sensory channel with its own processing logic rather than a copy of the cortical signal.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Individual lateral amygdala neurons receive convergent inputs from auditory thalamus (fast, ~12ms latency) and auditory cortex (slow, ~22ms latency). Thalamic transmission requires both AMPA and NMDA receptors; cortical transmission uses only AMPA. NMDA involvement gives the thalamic synapse temporal integration capacity and Hebbian plasticity properties not present in the cortical synapse.
mapping.ree_translation: ARC-027: the parallel nature of the HARM stream is not merely anatomical redundancy — the thalamic route has distinct computational properties (NMDA-mediated temporal integration) vs the cortical/E1 route (AMPA-only, faster but less integrative). In REE implementation terms, this implies the harm-detection module should have its own feature integration capacity, not simply receive z_world after E1 processing. The NMDA-like temporal integration at the thalamo-amygdala synapse parallels a separate learned harm-detector that accumulates evidence over time rather than responding to instantaneous world-state.
mapping.mapping_caveat: This paper focuses on auditory fear conditioning machinery. The nociceptive analog would involve the spinothalamic tract → VPM/Po → lateral amygdala synapse, which has not been directly tested for differential receptor use in this study. The receptor mechanism claim is well-established for the auditory circuit; the generalization to nociception is plausible but not directly evidenced here.
mapping.source_context: In vivo intracellular and extracellular recording study. Uses iontophoretic application of NMDA antagonist APV and AMPA antagonist CNQX. Key finding: MK-801 (systemic NMDA blocker) selectively disrupts thalamic but not cortical pathway input to lateral amygdala cells.

confidence_components.notes: High quality electrophysiology study. Mapping to ARC-027 is strong for the 'mechanistically distinct parallel channels' claim. Higher transfer risk than Romanski & LeDoux 1992 because the receptor-mechanism argument requires bridging from auditory to nociceptive circuits.
